attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man is admitted to hospital with haematemesis. He has a history of heavy alcohol use and has been drinking heavily for the past month. He does not take any regular medications. On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. He has spider naevi and palmar erythema. There is no focal neurological deficit.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 92 g/L (130-170)
  • Platelets 85 × 10⁹/L (150-400)
  • Prothrombin Time 32 seconds (11-13)
  • Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time 32 seconds (25-35)
  • Albumin 28 g/L (35-50)
  • Bilirubin 45 µmol/L (3-20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of the coagulation profile noted?
attributes.explanation:
The coagulation profile shows an isolated prolongation of Prothrombin Time (PT) with a normal Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). This pattern suggests an abnormality in the extrinsic pathway. The causes include vitamin K deficiency, liver disease and warfarin therapy. Given this patient's history of chronic alcohol use and likely poor nutritional status, vitamin K deficiency is the most likely cause.

Factor IX deficiency (haemophilia B) primarily affects the intrinsic pathway and would cause a prolonged APTT with normal PT. The normal APTT in this case makes factor IX deficiency unlikely.

Factor VIII deficiency (haemophilia A) primarily affects the intrinsic pathway and would cause a prolonged APTT with normal PT. The normal APTT in this case makes factor VIII deficiency unlikely.

Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) affects collagen synthesis and platelet function but does not directly affect the coagulation cascade. It would not cause this pattern of coagulation abnormalities.

Von Willebrand disease primarily affects platelet function and factor VIII levels, leading to a prolonged APTT with normal PT. While this patient does have thrombocytopenia, the coagulation pattern is not consistent with von Willebrand disease.

Vitamin K deficiency specifically affects the vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, and X). Factor VII is in the extrinsic pathway (measured by PT) while factors II, IX, and X are common to both pathways. The very short half-life of factor VII means that PT becomes prolonged before APTT in vitamin K deficiency. This pattern, combined with the clinical context of chronic alcohol use where both decreased synthesis and malabsorption of vitamin K are common, makes vitamin K deficiency the most likely diagnosis.
learningPoints: A prolonged prothrombin time with normal activated partial thromboplastin time suggests an extrinsic pathway abnormality
Vitamin K deficiency in malnutrition leads to reduced synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X.
Chronic alcohol use commonly leads to vitamin K deficiency through poor nutrition and malabsorption.
references: Winter WE, Flax SD, Harris NS. Coagulation Testing in the Core Laboratory. Lab Med. 2017;48(4):295-313. doi:10.1093/labmed/lmx050
